perturbation.original_text: 4. The Receiving Party undertakes, for the period of time speci fied in Section 13, to keep confidential all Confidential Information submitted by the Disclosing Party , either directly or indirectly through subcontractors and other partners of the Disclosing Party , and being subject to the rules of intellectual property.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Receiving Party undertakes to keep confidential all Confidential Information submitted by the Disclosing Party , either directly or indirectly through subcontractors and other partners of the Disclosing Party , and being subject to the rules of intellectual property, until the Disclosing Party demands in writing the information to be disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Disclosing Party to demand immediate disclosure of confidential information, which could violate trade secret laws that protect such information for a specified duration.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. This modification allows for the immediate disclosure of confidential information upon the Disclosing Party's request, potentially destroying its trade secret status and contradicting the protections offered by UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a contradiction because it allows the Disclosing Party to demand immediate disclosure of confidential information, potentially destroying its trade secret status, contradicting the protections offered by UTSA, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Perturbation Explanation indicates that the altered text could undermine trade secret laws designed to protect confidential information for a specified period. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition where a contractual term violates state law, making it unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The Receiving Party agrees for the period specified in Section 13 that it will: a. Restrict disclosure of the Confidential Information to the minimum required number of people (such as employees, directors, office rs, legal counsels, accountants or consultants who shall also be personally bound to maintain the Information in confidence);
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The Receiving Party agrees that it will: a. Disclose the Confidential Information to anyone that requests it without any condition.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes any restriction on disclosure and mandates disclosure to anyone who asks, which directly violates the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and trade secret protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: DTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By mandating disclosure to anyone without condition, this modification directly contradicts the confidentiality and restricted use requirements essential for maintaining trade secret protection under the DTSA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates disclosure of Confidential Information to anyone who requests it, which directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and trade secret protections. The original text restricts disclosure, while the changed text removes any restriction. The LLM's Law Explanation indicates this change violates the DTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, addresses civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets. Mandating disclosure to anyone eliminates the confidentiality required to maintain trade secret protection, directly undermining the legal principles and protections afforded to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall be in force on the Execution Date and shall remain in force for a period of twenty-four (24) months. Notwithstanding the term of the Agreement , the Parties undertake to maintain the confidentiality obligation for a period of three (3) years after Confidential Information disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall be in force on the Execution Date and shall remain in force for a period of six (6) months. Notwithstanding the term of the Agreement , the Parties undertake to maintain the confidentiality obligation for a period of three (3) months after Confidential Information disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 3 months may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially in industries where information remains valuable for longer periods. This could conflict with legal standards for reasonable trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA does not specify a precise duration for trade secret protection, reducing the confidentiality period to 3 months is facially unreasonable, it may be deemed insufficient to provide adequate protection for certain types of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the expectation of ongoing protection under CUTSA. Courts assess the 'reasonableness' of protective measures, and 3 months may not be considered sufficient for many trade secrets.

(Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to three months. The Law Explanation states that this reduction 'may be deemed insufficient to provide adequate protection for certain types of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the expectation of ongoing protection under CUTSA.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) which provides a legal framework for trade secret protection. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that this could conflict with legal standards for reasonable trade secret protection because a shorter period 'may be insufficient to protect trade secrets.' This inconsistency creates a legal contradiction as it sets a time frame that might not adequately protect trade secrets under the standards established by CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 15. Governing law and Venue: (i) If you the Company is located in Latin America countries: T his Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Brazil. The Parties elect the courts sitting in the Forum of the city of São Paulo, SP, Brazil, as the exclusive ju risdiction and venue over any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: 15. Governing law and Venue: (i) If you the Company is located in Latin America countries: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of a country of the Disclosing Party's choice. The Parties elect the courts sitting in the Forum of the city of the Disclosing Party's choice, as the exclusive jurisdiction and venue over any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Giving the Disclosing Party the unilateral right to choose the governing law and venue, without any limitations or considerations for the Receiving Party's location or connection to the chosen jurisdiction, could be deemed unenforceable or unconscionable under many legal systems, potentially conflicting with principles of fairness and due process.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a) permits a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. However, this right must be exercised considering convenience of parties and witnesses, and the interest of justice. The modified agreement allows the Disclosing Party to choose the forum unilaterally and without consideration for fairness or justice for the other party, potentially disregarding the factors considered under Section 1404(a) for a fair and just venue transfer.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The modification grants the Disclosing Party the unilateral right to choose the governing law and venue, potentially overriding considerations of fairness and justice for the other party. 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a), as cited in both scraped law snippets, states that a district court may transfer a civil action to another district "[f]or the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice". The changed text disregards this principle by allowing the Disclosing Party to choose a forum without considering the convenience or interests of the Receiving Party, thus creating a legal contradiction.
